food_subgroup: Bone-in mutton in coconut milk curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Kari Kambing. Closest parent is mutton/goat curry in coconut milk-based sauce. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mutton/goat curry in coconut milk-based curry sauce (Malay kari kambing style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0MEG used as closest parent for goat/mutton meat preparations; no exact Kari Kambing entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kari Kambing or Malay mutton curry as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Kari Kambing is approximately 250g, comprising bone-in mutton pieces with coconut milk gravy, as typically served at Malay nasi padang stalls or during festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Kari Kambing — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR), Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kari Kambing, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD for validation. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Meat-Based Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry entries; University of Sydney GI Database reference for meat-based curry (GI ~25)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kari Kambing in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 3.5g/100g, predominantly from spice paste and coconut milk. Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as: (25 × 3.5 × 250/100) / 100 = 2.2g, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 355mg/100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 888mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Kari Kambing to one serving per occasion and avoid adding extra salt or belacan, as a 250g serving contributes approximately 888mg sodium — roughly 39% of the 2,300mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.5g/100g); glycemic impact primarily from accompanying rice rather than the curry itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kari Kambing has a low glycemic index (~25) and minimal carbohydrate load from the curry itself, making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled and served without or with reduced rice; however, the high saturated fat (6.5g/100g) from coconut milk warrants moderation to avoid impairing insulin sensitivity with frequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.5g/100g from coconut milk and mutton fat; trans fat 0.2g/100g from ruminant source; cholesterol 88mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Kari Kambing to once per week, as a 250g serving delivers approximately 16.3g saturated fat — over 80% of the 20g daily limit — and should consider requesting leaner mutton cuts (leg or shoulder) and reduced coconut milk preparations to lower saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (6.5g/100g) and naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (0.2g/100g) may promote systemic inflammation; partially offset by anti-inflammatory spices
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Kari Kambing to once every two weeks, choose leaner cuts with less marbling, and consider replacing some coconut milk with a lighter stock; the galangal, cardamom, and cumin in the spice paste provide anti-inflammatory benefit that partially offsets the fat concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kari Kambing is the Malay-style mutton curry, slower-cooked and richer than its South Indian counterpart. It is distinguished by its full coconut milk base — which gives the gravy a creamy, white-yellow colour and a mild sweetness — and the deliberate absence of tamarind, unlike South Indian mutton curry. The slow simmer allows the mutton bones to release collagen and gelatin, giving the gravy a velvety, unctuous texture that coats the palate. The spice profile features warm whole spices — star anise, cardamom, cinnamon, and cumin — alongside a fresh paste of galangal, lemongrass, and chilli. It is a festive Malay dish traditionally served at weddings, Hari Raya celebrations, and Malay nasi padang stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, Kari Kambing is a high-protein (14.5g/100g), moderate-fat (12.8g/100g) dish with significant saturated fat (6.5g/100g) from coconut milk and mutton fat. It is notably zinc-rich (3.5mg/100g), supporting immune function and glucose metabolism. Sodium is moderate at 355mg/100g. Carbohydrate content is low (3.5g/100g), making the glycemic impact of the curry itself negligible; glycemic load is driven by accompanying rice. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management due to its saturated fat content, and suitable with moderation for Type 2 Diabetes given its low GI and minimal carbohydrate load.
common_user_queries: kari kambing vs Indian mutton curry

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kari Kambing, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2. The dish is a well-documented canonical Malay dish with consistent nutrient profiles across both sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kari Kambing not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g of 0.2g/100g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid from mutton), not industrially produced trans fat; this is confirmed per HPB source. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Kari Kambing exists. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (25 × 3.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact from the curry itself. All core macros (protein_g 14.5, fat_g 12.8, carbohydrate_g 3.5) are non-zero and sourced from verified HPB/IMR data.
